1: The angelic narrator (the messenger from Daniel 10) reports supporting Darius the Mede in his first year — the cosmic guardians of empires shape political stability. The verse bridges Daniel 10-11 and confirms the continuity of the angelic narrator across both chapters. This reinforces that the detailed prediction of Daniel 11-12 comes from the heavenly realm, not human political analysis.
2: Three more Persian kings, then a fourth — from the narrator's standpoint (Cyrus's third year, 536 BCE), three successors of Cyrus followed: Cambyses II (530-522 BCE), Smerdis/Bardiya (522 BCE, briefly), and Darius I Hystaspes (522-486 BCE). The fourth king, "far richer than all the others, who will stir up all against Greece," is Xerxes I (486-465 BCE) — the Ahasuerus of Esther, who launched the massive Persian invasion of Greece in 480 BCE immortalized at Thermopylae and Salamis. The description "with all his wealth" matches Herodotus's account of Xerxes's vast mobilization.
3: A powerful king who will do as he pleases — Alexander the Great (356-323 BCE), who conquered the Persian Empire in a decade of campaigning (334-323 BCE). The description "do as he pleases (ya'aseh kirtso'no)" is a consistent formula for self-willed absolute power in Daniel (Daniel 11:3, 16, 36; cf. Daniel 8:4), applied here to Alexander and later to the eschatological figure of Daniel 11:36.
4: Kingdom shattered and broken up toward the four winds — Alexander died in Babylon in June 323 BCE at age 32 without a legitimate heir (Roxane's son Alexander IV was posthumous). His empire was divided by the Diadochi (successors): primarily into the Ptolemaic kingdom (Egypt) and the Seleucid kingdom (Syria/Mesopotamia), with Macedon and Thrace going to others. The "four winds" division matches the four major successor kingdoms. "Not to his posterity" — the phrase specifically notes that his legitimate heirs (his half-brother Arrhidaeus, murdered 317 BCE, and son Alexander IV, murdered 310 BCE) did not retain power.
5: King of the south = Ptolemy I Soter (305-285 BCE), who established the Ptolemaic dynasty in Egypt. One of his commanders who will become greater = Seleucus I Nicator — Seleucus initially fled to Egypt after Antigonus drove him out of Babylon (316 BCE) and served under Ptolemy before recovering Babylon in 312 BCE and establishing the Seleucid empire, which at its height exceeded Ptolemy's territory.
6: An alliance through the king's daughter — ca. 252 BCE, Ptolemy II Philadelphus gave his daughter Berenice in marriage to Antiochus II Theos of the Seleucid kingdom, on the condition that Antiochus divorce his first wife Laodice. When Ptolemy II died, Antiochus took Laodice back, who then had Antiochus II poisoned, Berenice and her son killed, and placed her own son Seleucus II on the throne. This episode fulfilled the detail that "she will not retain her position of authority" — Berenice and her Egyptian entourage came to nothing.
7: One from her family line will arise = Berenice's brother Ptolemy III Euergetes (246-221 BCE), who invaded the Seleucid kingdom in the "Laodicean War" (246-241 BCE) in revenge for his sister's murder. He reached deep into Syria and Mesopotamia, plundering Seleucia-on-the-Tigris. "Enter the fortress (ma'oz) of the king of the north" — he captured the Seleucid capital Seleucia-on-the-Tigris and carried off enormous booty.
8: Ptolemy III returned to Egypt with "their gods, their metal images, and precious articles of silver and gold" — Polybius and other Greek sources confirm that he returned cult images that Cambyses had taken from Egypt three centuries earlier, making him enormously popular with the Egyptian priesthood (hence his title "Euergetes," benefactor). He survived longer than the king of the north (Seleucus II), as predicted.
9: Seleucus II's counterattack into Egypt (ca. 240 BCE) failed and he "retreated to his own country" — an exact correspondence to the text. The third Syrian War ended with no Seleucid gains; Seleucus II subsequently faced revolts in Asia Minor and died on campaign ca. 226 BCE.
10: His sons = Seleucus III (226-223 BCE) and Antiochus III "the Great" (223-187 BCE). Seleucus III campaigned but was assassinated; Antiochus III pursued the reconquest of Palestine and Coele-Syria. "Sweep forward like a flood" — Antiochus III's early campaigns recovered much Seleucid territory. "Will wage war as far as his fortress" — he pressed to the borders of Egypt.
11: Battle of Raphia (217 BCE) — Ptolemy IV Philopator marched out "furious" against Antiochus III's invasion. The Battle of Raphia (modern Rafah, Gaza-Egypt border) was a decisive Ptolemaic victory; ancient sources (Polybius 5.79-86) record Ptolemy's army of 70,000 infantry, 5,000 cavalry, and 73 elephants defeating Antiochus's slightly larger force. "The large army will be given into his hand" — Ptolemy IV won, as predicted.
12: Ptolemy IV's failure after Raphia — he became "arrogant" and did not press the victory to consolidate control. Contemporary sources describe his debauchery and mismanagement; he celebrated at Alexandria rather than annexing Syria. According to 3 Maccabees (historically questionable but reflecting the tradition), he sacrilegiously tried to enter the Jerusalem temple's inner precincts, was struck unconscious, and returned to Egypt without completing his advantage — consistent with "yet he will not maintain his ascendancy."
13: Antiochus III returns with a larger army — after Raphia (217 BCE), Antiochus III spent the next decade reconquering the eastern satrapies of the empire (the "Anabasis," 212-205 BCE), giving himself the epithet "the Great." With Ptolemy IV dead (204 BCE) and the child Ptolemy V on the throne, Antiochus allied with Philip V of Macedon and launched his reconquest of Coele-Syria/Palestine (203-198 BCE).
14: Violent men among your own people — the Hellenizing Jews who supported Antiochus III's takeover of Palestine from the Ptolemies in hopes of political advantage. Josephus (Antiquities 12.3.3) records that some Jews aided Antiochus III. "They will rebel against the vision" — their collaboration with imperial power ultimately failed to fulfill their ambitions; the Hellenizing project eventually produced the Maccabean crisis rather than Jewish privilege.
15: Capture of the well-fortified city — traditionally identified as Sidon (Polybius 16.18-19), which Antiochus III besieged and captured from the Ptolemaic general Scopas in 198 BCE. The "choice troops of the south" who could not resist = the Ptolemaic forces under Scopas. Antiochus III's victory at Panion (near modern Banias/Caesarea Philippi, 198 BCE) effectively ended Ptolemaic control of Palestine.
16: The Beautiful Land (eretz ha-tzevi) — the standard Daniel term for the Holy Land (cf. Daniel 8:9, Daniel 11:41). Antiochus III "established himself" in Judea ca. 198 BCE and issued decrees favorable to the Jews (Josephus, Antiquities 12.3.3-4), granting tax relief and supporting the temple cult — a stark contrast to what his son Antiochus IV would do 30 years later. "All of it in his power to destroy" — he had unchallenged control over the Beautiful Land.
17: His daughter to the king of the south — Antiochus III gave his daughter Cleopatra I as wife to the young Ptolemy V (ca. 196 BCE) as part of the Peace of Lysimacheia, retaining nominal sovereignty over Coele-Syria. His hope that she would act as a Seleucid intelligence asset backfired — "but his plan will not succeed, nor will it help him," as Cleopatra sided with her husband Ptolemy V and even supported Rome against her own father. The scheme to "undermine the realm of Egypt through her" failed completely.
18: Turn his attention to the coastlands — after consolidating his eastern and Palestinian gains, Antiochus III turned westward, campaigning in Asia Minor and even landing in Greece (192 BCE). A commander will put an end to his insolence = the Roman general Lucius Cornelius Scipio Asiaticus (brother of Scipio Africanus), who defeated Antiochus III decisively at the Battle of Magnesia in Lydia (190 BCE). The Romans were not yet a named power in Daniel's framework; they appear obliquely here and more directly in Daniel 11:30 ("ships of Kittim").
19: After Magnesia, Antiochus III was forced to surrender his fleet, his war elephants, and territory west of the Taurus mountains (Treaty of Apamea, 188 BCE). He returned east ("fortresses of his own country") and was killed at Elymais in Persia (187 BCE) while trying to plunder the temple of Bel at Elymais to pay the Roman war indemnity. "He will stumble and fall, to be seen no more" — the most powerful Seleucid ruler died ignominiously attempting temple robbery.
20: A tax collector through the prestigious part of the kingdom = Seleucus IV Philopator (187-175 BCE), who sent his minister Heliodorus to plunder the Jerusalem temple treasury to raise Roman tribute money (2 Macc Daniel 3:1-40). Heliodorus was supernaturally prevented (the tradition in 2 Macc 3 of a heavenly horseman). Seleucus IV was subsequently poisoned by Heliodorus himself ("destroyed, yet not in anger or in battle") in 175 BCE.
21: A contemptible person — Antiochus IV Epiphanes (175-164 BCE), younger son of Antiochus III. He was not the legitimate heir; Seleucus IV's son Demetrius was the rightful heir (then held hostage in Rome). Antiochus seized power through flattery (chalaqalot) of the Seleucid nobility and allies, not by traditional succession. He gave himself the title Epiphanes (God Manifest); opponents called him Epimanes (the Mad One). His treatment of the Jerusalem high priesthood (purchasing the office for the highest bidder — Jason replacing Onias III in 175 BCE, then Menelaus replacing Jason in 172 BCE) was the first major act of covenant desecration in his reign.
22: Prince of the covenant will be shattered — widely identified as Onias III, the last legitimate high priest, who was deposed and ultimately murdered ca. 171 BCE (2 Macc Daniel 4:33-38). The high priest was the "prince of the covenant" in the sense that he presided over Israel's covenant worship; his removal and murder marked the end of the legitimate Zadokite high priesthood. An "overwhelming army" was swept away by Antiochus IV's military campaigns in Egypt.
23: Antiochus IV came to power with "only a small force" — he initially ruled with limited military resources, using political cunning before building up strength. His deceit and intrigue against both the Seleucid nobility and the Jewish leadership are well-documented in 1-2 Maccabees. The "alliance" may refer to his initial cooperative relationship with Ptolemy VI Philometor before the conflict of 170-168 BCE.
24: "Without warning he will invade the most productive areas" — Antiochus IV's practice of sudden, unexpected invasion, distributing plunder to buy loyalty, was noted by ancient writers. The phrase "accomplish what his ancestors never did" — his willingness to desecrate temples (ultimately the Jerusalem temple) and break all customary boundaries went beyond even his predecessors. "For a time" (ad et) — the temporal limit is built into the prediction; his power has a divinely appointed terminus.
25: First Egyptian campaign (170-169 BCE) — Antiochus IV invaded Egypt using Ptolemy VI Philometor's advisors' treachery as a pretext (they had actually incited the conflict). He defeated the Egyptian army near Pelusium and occupied much of Egypt, including Memphis, leaving Ptolemy VI as a puppet. The "large army raised against him" refers to the Ptolemaic forces that were outmaneuvered and defeated.
26: Ptolemy VI was undermined by those of "his own table" — his advisors and inner court who proved disloyal or incompetent. The defeat of the Egyptian army is confirmed by Polybius and Livy. Antiochus IV effectively controlled Egypt except for Alexandria, which held out behind the Ptolemaic navy.
27: The famous meeting of Antiochus IV and Ptolemy VI at which "both kings will tell lies to each other" — Polybius (28.21) describes diplomatic negotiations between the two at Memphis that both knew to be insincere. Ptolemy VI secretly sought Roman intervention while appearing to cooperate with Antiochus; Antiochus maintained the pretense of acting as Ptolemy VI's protector while pursuing his own annexation. "But to no avail" — neither king achieved his objective at this meeting.
28: Returning through Judea — on his return from Egypt (169 BCE), Antiochus IV plundered the Jerusalem temple, taking "the golden altar, the lampstand, and all its vessels" (1 Macc Daniel 1:21-24). This was the first major desecration of the temple. "His heart set against the holy covenant" — the temple plunder was not incidental but reflected ideological hostility to the covenant community. He "returned to his own country" before the climactic desecration of 167 BCE.
29: Second Egyptian campaign (168 BCE) — Antiochus IV invaded Egypt again, this time with greater force, intending permanent annexation. However, "this time the outcome will be different from what it was before" — Rome was now directly involved.
30: Ships of Kittim — in the OT, Kittim originally referred to Cyprus (Numbers 24:24; Isaiah 23:1), but by the second century BCE it was used for Rome in Jewish texts (the Dead Sea Scrolls War Scroll consistently uses "Kittim" for Rome). This verse describes the famous confrontation of 168 BCE at Alexandria: the Roman senator Gaius Popillius Laenas presented Antiochus IV with the Senate's ultimatum to withdraw from Egypt. When Antiochus asked for time to consider, Popillius drew a circle in the sand around Antiochus and demanded an answer before he stepped out of it. Antiochus submitted, withdrawing from Egypt entirely. "He will turn back and vent his fury against the holy covenant" — the humiliation at Rome's hands redirected his aggression toward Judea.
31: The abomination of desolation — December 167 BCE: Antiochus IV's forces (under his general Apollonius according to 1 Macc Daniel 1:29-35) garrisoned Jerusalem, abolished the tamid (daily morning and evening sacrifice), and erected an altar to Olympian Zeus on the altar of burnt offering. 1 Macc 1:54 calls this the "abomination of desolation"; 2 Macc 6:2 adds that the temple was renamed the sanctuary of Zeus Olympios. Pigs were sacrificed on the altar. This event directly triggered the Maccabean revolt (1 Macc Daniel 2:1-28). Jesus cites this verse explicitly in Matthew 24:15, indicating it has a further eschatological referent beyond Antiochus IV.
32: Corrupt those who violate the covenant — the Hellenizing Jews (the Tobiads, the high priests Jason and Menelaus) who collaborated with Antiochus IV and adopted Greek customs, abandoning circumcision and the Torah (1 Macc Daniel 1:11-15). People who know their God will stand firm — the Hasidim ("pious ones"), a movement of Torah-faithful Jews who formed the core of the Maccabean resistance. The Hasidim are the probable ancestors of both the Pharisees and the Essenes (Dead Sea Scrolls community).
33: The wise will instruct many (maskilei am yavinu le-rabbim) — the teachers of the covenant community. The catalogue of suffering — sword, burning, captivity, plunder — corresponds directly to the persecution under Antiochus IV documented in 1-2 Maccabees: thousands were killed, families were slaughtered for observing the Sabbath or circumcising sons (1 Macc Daniel 1:60-64). The "little help" may refer to the Maccabean revolt under Mattathias and his sons — effective but ultimately limited; it purified the temple but did not bring the eschatological kingdom.
34: "Many who join them will do so insincerely" (venilavim alehem bachalaqlot) — in every revival or movement of faithfulness, opportunists join for advantage rather than conviction. The Maccabean movement attracted both the deeply faithful and those who joined for political or material reasons. The acknowledgment that the community of resistance is not pure does not invalidate the movement; God works through the mixed community of the partially faithful.
35: The stumbling of the wise "to refine, purify, and cleanse" them (labreram u-lalbenim u-latsaraph) — the same triple metallurgical language as Daniel 12:10. The suffering of the faithful is not meaningless but has a refining purpose. "Until the time of the end (ad et qetz)" — this phrase introduces an eschatological horizon that some interpreters see as signaling a transition from the Maccabean historical period to a future fulfillment in Daniel 11:36-45.
36: A transition point — most scholars note that beginning here, the description does not easily map onto the historical career of Antiochus IV. Antiochus IV died at Tabae in Persia (164 BCE) while besieged at Elymais, having failed to plunder a temple; he died "in anger and in battle" in one sense, but not "between the sea and the holy mountain" (Daniel 11:45) as predicted. Many interpreters therefore see a transition at Daniel 11:36 to an eschatological figure — either a future Antichrist figure (Christian/Jewish eschatological tradition) or a symbolic composite. The description "exalting himself above every god, speaking unheard-of things against the God of gods" has parallels in 2 Thessalonians 2:4 and Revelation 13:5-6.
37: No regard for the gods of his ancestors or for the one desired by women — "gods of his ancestors" is debated: if read of Antiochus IV, it may refer to his neglect of traditional Seleucid gods in favor of Zeus Olympios. "The one desired by women" (chemdat nashim) — variously interpreted as Tammuz/Adonis (a dying-and-rising deity whose cult was practiced by women; cf. Ezekiel 8:14) or as a general reference to family or conjugal affection. The verse describes comprehensive rejection of all prior religious and relational loyalties.
38: God of fortresses (Elohei ma'oz) — the deity Antiochus IV honored above all others. In Roman tradition, Antiochus IV was known for his devotion to Jupiter Capitolinus (Zeus Olympios), whom he identified with his own divine manifestation (Epiphanes). The consistent identification with military fortress-power fits his use of military force as the instrument of his will and his cult.
39: Distributing land as reward to those who "acknowledge" the foreign god — Antiochus IV's practice of giving land grants to loyal supporters who adopted Hellenistic cult practices. This was a systematic program of using land redistribution to incentivize religious apostasy among the Jewish elite (cf. 1 Macc Daniel 2:18, where Mattathias is offered such incentives).
40: At the time of the end (be-et qetz) — the phrase marks the eschatological timeframe. Whether this describes Antiochus IV's final campaigns or a future eschatological figure, the geography is clear: a conflict between the "king of the south" and the "king of the north" that reaches the Holy Land. If read of Antiochus IV, it anticipates a final Egyptian campaign and Judean invasion that does not match his historical death in Persia.
41: The Beautiful Land = Judea (see Daniel 11:16). Edom, Moab, and the leaders of Ammon escape — historically, these Transjordanian nations had various accommodations with Antiochus IV and the Seleucid power. If read eschatologically, they represent nations that receive temporary reprieve at the end of the age. The specific geography is striking: the prediction explicitly names three peoples east of the Jordan who are not conquered.
42: Egypt does not escape — "he will extend his power over many countries" including Egypt. The contrast with Daniel 11:25-30 (where Antiochus IV reached Egypt but was expelled by Rome) reinforces the sense that Daniel 11:40-45 describe a different scenario than the 168 BCE campaign.
43: Treasures of gold and silver with Libya and Cush "at his steps" — control of Egypt's wealth and the submission of the North African nations. This level of Egyptian domination exceeds what Antiochus IV historically achieved. Libya (H3864 Luvim) and Cush (H3568, Ethiopia/Sudan) represent the western and southern extent of the Ptolemaic sphere of influence.
44: "Reports from the east and the north will alarm him" — something draws the king away from his Egyptian campaign, and he responds with extreme violence. Historically, Antiochus IV's last campaign was eastward (into Persia/Elymais), not from the east northward. This verse's geography is one of the textual details that makes simple identification with Antiochus IV's historical career difficult.
45: Royal tents between the sea and the beautiful holy mountain = the Mediterranean coast and Mount Zion/Jerusalem — this geographic staging is the final position of the eschatological adversary before his end. Yet he will come to his end, and no one will help him — the abrupt divine termination of the power that seemed unstoppable. Antiochus IV died at Tabae (Persia, 164 BCE), not in Palestine; if this verse is read of him, it represents unfulfilled prediction requiring reinterpretation; if read eschatologically, it describes the final end of the end-time adversary. The connection to Daniel 12:1-2 is immediate: after this end, "Michael will arise" and the resurrection occurs.
